tavily.answer: Nearly 20 million people in Sudan are facing acute hunger due to civil war, collapsing infrastructure, and worsening insecurity, pushing the country into one of the world’s gravest humanitarian disasters. The UN-backed Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) reports that more than 40% of Sudan’s population is struggling with acute food insecurity after three years of fighting between the Sudanese Armed Forces and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces. A Sudanese medical group accused the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces of killing 27 civilians, including elderly people, in North Kordofan villages during Eid al-Adha, in an area free of any military presence. The crisis is expected to worsen as drone attacks intensify, humanitarian access shrinks, and the rainy season threatens food supplies across conflict-hit regions.

acled.note: Your ACLED access tier serves data up to 2025-07-08 (~12-month embargo).

roads.answer: The ongoing crisis in Sudan has led to significant disruptions, including road closures, blocked highways, destroyed bridges, checkpoints, border crossing closures, and impassable evacuation routes. The United Nations has warned of a catastrophe unfolding in Sudan's al-Obeid, with reports of atrocities and a potential human rights disaster. The U.S. State Department has expressed concern over the Rapid Support Forces massing near El Obeid, warning of possible mass atrocities if the city falls to the paramilitary group. The situation poses a significant challenge for the Abraham Accords states, including Israel, which has a moral obligation to address Sudan's crisis.
